## Presentation Rubric: Oral Delivery

1. Structure: Was the presentation *well structured*, stating a relevant thesis, supporting it with argument(s) that are clearly related to this point and (if relevant) distinct from one another, and linking these arguments in a logical way?
2. Argumentation: Did the presentation do a *good job of making its case*, choosing relevant arguments, backing them up with evidence and examples at an appropriate level of detail, and responding to contrary views as appropriate?
3. Subject matter: Did the presentation demonstrate a good *understanding of the course's subject matter*, including both the topic and the wider context?
4. English: Was the presentation presented *clearly and in correct English*?

| Criterion | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **1. Structure** | Presentation is **well structured**, with a clear purpose, logically organized sections, and a strong progression of ideas. Each part supports the overall message, and transitions effectively connect the presentation. | Presentation has a clear purpose and generally follows a logical structure. Most sections contribute to the overall message, with only minor issues in organization or transitions. | Presentation has a recognizable structure, but ideas are not always clearly linked. Some sections may be redundant, poorly connected, or insufficiently developed. | Presentation has a basic structure but struggles to maintain a logical progression. Logical progression of argument(s) and counterargument(s) is present but some content is irrelevant and/or redundant. | It is possible to understand the presentation despite the lack of clear organization, but there is little logical progression or coherent structure. Logical progression of argument(s) and counterargument(s) is unclear or unrelated. | No presentation, non-English presentation, or substantially incomplete presentation (e.g. only an outline). |
| **2. Argumentation** | Whether or not I personally agree with the presentation's conclusions, it makes a compelling argument for its point of view. Arguments are very relevant, backed up with evidence at an appropriate level of detail, and strong responses are offered to relevant counterarguments or contrary evidence. | Arguments are clearly stated and generally support the main message; these arguments are backed up with generally relevant evidence at a broadly appropriate level of detail. Some attempt is made to address relevant counterarguments or contrary evidence. | Presentation makes recognizable arguments and backs them up with evidence, but relevance and/or level of detail are very inappropriate. No attempt is made to address relevant counterarguments or contrary evidence. | The presentation stated many facts about the topic in question, but it is not possible to determine what are arguments, evidence, or opinions. | The presentation provides mostly irrelevant information or unsupported opinions that do not address the central message. | No presentation, non-English presentation, or substantially incomplete presentation (e.g. only an outline). |
| **3. Subject Matter** | The presentation contains arguments, evidence, and concepts that broaden the scope of understanding, demonstrating creative and insightful understanding of the topic alongside balanced use of the course's subject matter. | Presentation contains relevant course materials, demonstrating generally correct or reasonable understanding of the material and topic. No significant errors are present. | Presentation contains mostly relevant course materials but makes some incorrect or inaccurate statements and/or interpretations or includes some errors of omission (failure to introduce important facts). Personal opinions may be presented as fact. | Presentation contains some relevant course materials but makes many incorrect or inaccurate statements and/or interpretations. Personal opinions may be presented as fact. | Presentation does not contain any relevant course material including frameworks, theories, evidence, etc. | No presentation, non-English presentation, or substantially incomplete presentation (e.g. only an outline). |
| **4. Communication** | The presentation communicates ideas clearly, confidently, and concisely. Language is appropriate for the audience, visuals effectively support the presentation, and delivery is polished. Minimal errors may be present and can be easily ignored. | The presentation communicates ideas clearly with only minor issues in delivery, language, or visuals that do not affect understanding. | The presentation is understandable but requires some effort due to delivery, language, pacing, or visual design. May include communication that is inappropriate for the audience and/or topic. | The presentation is difficult to understand because of frequent communication problems, poor delivery, or ineffective visuals. | The presentation is nearly impossible to understand despite significant effort. |  No presentation, non-English presentation, or substantially incomplete presentation (e.g. only an outline). |

### Grade Summary

| Criterion | Marks |
|:---|---:|
| Structure | /5 |
| Argumentation | /5 |
| Subject Matter | /5 |
| Communication | /5 |
| **Total** | **/20** |
